artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down after getting the kids to bed and wrangling Max back in from the yard (he found yet another mud puddle). I wanted to update you on what’s been going on with Ethan this spring, because I know you worry.

You remember last April during the MCA math testing window at Lakeview Middle School when he had that full-blown panic attack in homeroom? The school called it a “medical episode,” but it was terrifying. That’s what pushed us to start seeing Dr. Andrea Holm at the Woodbury Pediatric & Adolescent Mental Health Clinic. She officially diagnosed him with generalized anxiety disorder on May 6, 2024 and, in her treatment plan, she actually recommended that he avoid all high‑stakes standardized testing for at least 12 months.

On top of that, back in April–May 2023 the pressure around the MCAs was part of what led to starting sertraline with Dr. Michael Nguyen at the M Health Fairview clinic in Woodbury. His anxiety and mood tanked during that testing stretch, and I really don’t want to go through another round of med tweaks just because of test scores.

He’s doing well day to day now—he likes his English class, and robotics club has been a bright spot. He still gets nervous before quizzes, but it’s nothing like those marathon testing days. Dan keeps working with him on math in the evenings, and Abby loves when he helps her practice spelling words at the dining table.

We’re still planning that long weekend up to Duluth to see Grandma Susan and walk along Canal Park—probably in October if Dan can work around conferences. I’ll let you know dates so you and Dad can maybe come over to Woodbury and dog‑sit Max.

Love,
Melissa

MELISSA: Hey, can you talk for a sec or are you in the middle of bedtime chaos?

SISTER: Kids are brushing teeth. What’s up?

MELISSA: Just needed to vent about school stuff. Ethan’s already stressing about spring, even though we told him he’s not doing the MCAs this year.

SISTER: Ugh. What set it off?

MELISSA: He remembered last April during the MCA reading test at Lakeview when Mr. Jensen told the class that kids who didn’t hit “proficient” might end up in remedial groups next year. Ethan basically froze. It totally triggered that old fear of being labeled, like what happened with Tyler at Harding High after the October 2022 suspension and the emotional‑behavioral IEP. He still brings that up.

SISTER: Yeah, that whole Harding mess really stuck with him.

MELISSA: Exactly. And then there was this past April 9, 2024 during MCA math—his anxiety ramped up so badly that Ms. Ramirez, the counselor at Lakeview, called me at Medisphere in the middle of my client presentation about that $250,000 contract. I had to walk out halfway through the demo. My manager pulled me aside later and basically said another incident like that could show up in my performance review.

SISTER: Seriously??

MELISSA: Yep. So between that, Dr. Holm’s recommendation, and everything with Tyler, we’re done. Dan keeps telling Ethan the tests are mostly for the district anyway, not a judgment on him. Honestly, I agree.

SISTER: I don’t blame you at all.

MELISSA: Anyway, on a lighter note, are you free Saturday? Thinking of bringing the kids into St. Paul for the fall festival near Harding and maybe meeting up for mini donuts.

Hey Jenna,

Catching up while Ethan is at robotics and Abby is allegedly doing homework (which probably means drawing dragons instead of math). Thought I’d loop you in on our spring break plans and the latest kid drama.

We decided we’re not having Ethan do the state MCAs this year. After everything the last couple of springs—panic attack during the April 2024 MCA math window at Lakeview, the ER scare, weekly therapy, the whole nine yards—it just feels like too much. Remember when we ended up at Regions Hospital’s pediatric ER on March 3, 2024 because he had chest pain the night before those MCA practice tests? They did an EKG to rule out any cardiac issues (thankfully normal) and the doctor basically said it was a massive anxiety episode. That’s what led us to start weekly sessions at Nystrom & Associates in Woodbury later in March specifically for test‑related anxiety.

He’s actually doing better now—still a worrier, but functioning. And honestly, not having to work around the testing schedule makes it a lot easier for us to sneak in a family trip that week. We’re thinking of driving up to Duluth, seeing Dan’s mom, and letting the kids burn energy walking along Canal Park and maybe doing the aquarium.

Work is the usual circus at Medisphere. I’m still juggling a couple of big healthcare tech accounts in St. Paul, but at least no one has had to call me out of a client demo lately. Dan’s deep into planning his East Ridge civics unit, and Ethan keeps waffling between “future coder” and “future zookeeper.”

Anyway, if your crew is around the first weekend of break, maybe we can do a joint game night when we’re back.

– Melissa

Personal note – for my own reference about Ethan’s anxiety/testing history so I don’t lose track of the timeline when I’m talking with providers or teachers.

– April–May 2023: MCA season was rough. Pressure around scores ramped up his anxiety and low mood. That’s when we started sertraline with Dr. Michael Nguyen at the M Health Fairview Clinic in Woodbury. Several med checks that spring because the testing stress seemed to make everything worse.

– March 3, 2024: Scary night. Ethan had chest pain the evening before scheduled MCA practice tests. We took him to Regions Hospital pediatric ER in St. Paul. They ran an EKG to rule out cardiac problems; results were normal, but they flagged anxiety as the likely cause.

– March 2024 (later that month): Began weekly therapy at Nystrom & Associates in Woodbury focusing on test‑related anxiety and coping skills.

– April 2024: During the MCA math testing window at Lakeview Middle School, he had a full‑blown panic attack in homeroom. After that, we increased check‑ins with the school counselor, Ms. Ramirez.

– May 6, 2024: First full evaluation with Dr. Andrea Holm at Woodbury Pediatric & Adolescent Mental Health Clinic. She formally diagnosed generalized anxiety disorder and recommended avoiding all high‑stakes standardized testing for at least 12 months.

– April 9, 2024: Separate but related—during another MCA math session, Ms. Ramirez called me at work at Medisphere in the middle of a $250,000 client presentation because Ethan was melting down.

Overall: He’s now in 7th grade (2025–2026) at Lakeview, doing well academically with regular quizzes/projects. We’re prioritizing mental health and non‑high‑stakes assessments.

Draft – not sent
To: principal.lakeview@isd833.org
Subject: Concerns about spring testing

Dear Principal [Last Name],

I’m writing as Ethan Carlson’s mom (7th grade, Lakeview Middle) to put some thoughts in one place about the way spring testing has played out for him over the last few years.

Last April, during the MCA reading test, his homeroom teacher Mr. Jensen told the class that students who didn’t reach “proficient” might end up in remedial groups the following year. I understand the intent may have been to motivate, but for Ethan it had the opposite effect. He immediately thought of his cousin Tyler at Harding High School in St. Paul, who ended up with an IEP for emotional‑behavioral disorders after a suspension in October 2022. Ethan has a deep fear of being singled out or labeled like that, and he basically shut down for the rest of the session.

Layer that on top of the medical and mental‑health fallout we’ve seen—panic attack during the April 2024 MCA math window, the March 3, 2024 ER visit at Regions Hospital’s pediatric unit for chest pain the night before MCA practice tests (EKG thankfully normal), weekly therapy at Nystrom & Associates in Woodbury, and the generalized anxiety diagnosis from Dr. Andrea Holm at Woodbury Pediatric & Adolescent Mental Health Clinic—and it’s hard not to feel disheartened.

I know the district emphasizes participation, but we’re honestly at the point where refusing the tests feels like the only way to say we’re fed up with how much time and emotional energy they consume.

Sincerely,
Melissa Carlson

(Leaving this in drafts until I cool off.)

metadata.subject: Draft – concerns about MCAs and Ethan

artifacts.content:
Event: Ethan – weekly therapy (test anxiety)
Date: Mondays, recurring (start: 2024-03-18)
Time: 4:00–4:50 p.m.
Location: Nystrom & Associates, Woodbury, MN
Attendees: Ethan, therapist

Notes:
– Started these weekly sessions in March 2024 after the scare on March 3, 2024 when we took Ethan to Regions Hospital pediatric ER in St. Paul for chest pain the night before MCA practice tests. ER ran an EKG to rule out cardiac issues; results normal, anxiety suspected.
– Focus of therapy: coping strategies for test‑related anxiety, especially around long standardized sessions like the MCA math and reading windows at Lakeview Middle School.
– Remember to mention to therapist: the April 2024 MCA math panic attack in homeroom, the April 9, 2024 incident when Ms. Ramirez called me out of my Medisphere HealthTech client presentation about the $250,000 contract, and how Ethan still talks about Mr. Jensen’s “remedial groups” comment during the MCA reading test and his fear of ending up like cousin Tyler at Harding High with the emotional‑behavioral IEP after the October 2022 suspension.
– Bring updates from Dr. Andrea Holm (Woodbury Pediatric & Adolescent Mental Health Clinic, GAD diagnosis on 5/6/24) and from Dr. Michael Nguyen at M Health Fairview in Woodbury regarding sertraline and any dosage adjustments.
– Practical: leave Willow Bend Drive by 3:35 p.m., factor in after‑school traffic from Lakeview. Pack a snack in Ethan’s backpack and his clarinet if it’s a band day.

Subject: Yesterday + testing stuff

Dan,

Just wanted to debrief about yesterday now that the house is quiet and Ethan’s finally asleep.

Ms. Ramirez called me again from Lakeview in the middle of the day—this time it was just a smaller quiz, not MCAs, but Ethan started to spiral because someone mentioned “benchmark data.” It immediately brought him back to last April’s MCA math session and that full‑blown panic attack in homeroom. I keep thinking about how, on April 9, 2024, she had to call me at Medisphere during the Medisphere–NorthStar Health demo about the $250,000 contract and I walked out of the room with my heart in my throat. I can still feel my manager’s “another incident like this could be a problem” talk.

On top of that, he’s still jittery whenever anyone says “reading group” because of what Mr. Jensen told them during the April 2024 MCA reading test—that kids who didn’t hit “proficient” might end up in remedial groups. It taps right into his fear of being labeled like Tyler at Harding after the October 2022 suspension and the emotional‑behavioral IEP.

I know we’ve already agreed he’s not doing the MCAs this year, and with Dr. Holm’s GAD diagnosis from May 6, 2024 and the 12‑month recommendation to avoid high‑stakes testing, I feel solid about that. Plus, we’ve got Nystrom on Mondays and Dr. Nguyen at M Health Fairview keeping an eye on the sertraline.

Logistics: can you handle soccer drop‑off at Bielenberg tomorrow so I can get Abby to her Oak Meadow event? I’ll do bus duty in the morning and make oatmeal.

– M

Notes to self:

- Received Information And Opt Out: true
- Mca Mtas Reading: true
- Mca Mtas Science: true
- Mca Mtas Mathematics: true
- Access Or Alternate Access For Ells: false
